analogy: Think of "Principles of Inheritance" through the chapter's own opening images: CHAPTER 5 MOLECULAR BASIS OF INHERITANCE 5.1 The DNA 5.2 The Search for Genetic Material 5.3 RNA World 5.4 Replication 5.5 Transcription 5.6 Genetic Code 5.7 Translation 5.8 Regulation of Gene Expression 5.9 Human Genome Project 5.10 DNA Fingerprinting In the previous chapter, you have... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: CHAPTER 5, MOLECULAR BASIS OF, INHERITANCE, 5.1 The DNA. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Principles of Inheritance is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that cHAPTER 5 MOLECULAR BASIS OF INHERITANCE 5.1 The DNA 5.2 The Search for Genetic Material 5.3 RNA World 5.4 Replication 5.5 Transcription 5.6 Genetic Code 5.7 Translation 5.8 Regulation of Gene Expression 5.9 Human Genome Project 5.10 DNA Fingerprinting In the previous chapter, you have learnt the inheritance patterns and the genetic basis of such patterns.

The text explains that at the time of Mendel, the nature of those ‘factors’ regulating the pattern of inheritance was not clear.

The text explains that over the next hundred years, the nature of the putative genetic material was investigated culminating in the realisation that DNA – deoxyribonucleic acid – is the genetic material, at least for the majority of organisms.

The text explains that in class XI you have learnt that nucleic acids are polymers of nucleotides.

The text explains that deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) and ribonucleic acid (RNA) are the two types of nucleic acids found in living systems.

The text explains that dNA acts as the genetic material in most of the organisms.

The text explains that rNA though it also acts as a genetic material in some viruses, mostly functions as a messenger.

The text explains that it functions as adapter, structural, and in some cases as a catalytic molecule.

The text explains that in Class XI you have already learnt the structures of nucleotides and the way these monomer units are linked to form nucleic acid polymers.

The text explains that in this chapter we are going to discuss the structure of DNA, its replication, the process of making RNA from DNA (transcription), the genetic code that determines the sequences of amino acids in proteins, the process of protein synthesis (translation) and elementary basis of their regulation.

The text explains that 80 BIOLOGY of complete nucleotide sequence of human genome during last decade has set in a new era of genomics.

The text explains that in the last section, the essentials of human genome sequencing and its consequences will also be discussed.

The text explains that let us begin our discussion by first understanding the structure of the most interesting molecule in the living system, that is, the DNA.

The text explains that in subsequent sections, we will understand that why it is the most abundant genetic material, and what its relationship is with RNA.

The text explains that 5.1 THE DNA DNA is a long polymer of deoxyribonucleotides.

The text explains that the length of DNA is usually defined as number of nucleotides (or a pair of nucleotide referred to as base pairs) present in it.

The text explains that let us discuss the structure of such a long polymer.

The text explains that 5.1.1 Structure of Polynucleotide Chain Let us recapitulate the chemical structure of a polynucleotide chain (DNA or RNA).

The text explains that however, due to technical limitation in isolating such a long polymer intact, the elucidation of structure of DNA remained elusive for a very long period of time.

The text explains that it was only in 1953 that James Watson and Francis Crick, based on the X-ray diffraction data produced by Maurice Wilkins and Rosalind Franklin, proposed a very simple but famous Double Helix model for the structure of DNA.

The text explains that because of this, the genetic implications of the structure of DNA became very clear.

The text explains that the salient features of the Double-helix structure of DNA are as follows: (i) It is made of two polynucleotide chains, where the backbone is constituted by sugar-phosphate, and the bases project inside.

The text explains that (iv) The two chains are coiled in a right-handed fashion.

The text explains that this, in addition to H-bonds, confers stability of the helical structure (Figure 5.3).

The text explains that compare the structure of purines and pyrimidines.

The text explains that the proposition of a double helix structure for DNA and its simplicity in explaining the genetic implication became revolutionary.

The text explains that 83 MOLECULAR BASIS OF INHERITANCE Figure 5.4a Nucleosome Figure 5.4b EM picture - ‘Beads-on-String’ In some viruses the flow of information is in reverse direction, that is, from RNA to DNA.

The text explains that can you suggest a simple name to the process?

The text explains that the negatively charged DNA is wrapped around the positively charged histone octamer to form a structure called nucleosome (Figure 5.4 a).

The text explains that nucleosomes constitute the repeating unit of a structure in nucleus called chromatin, thread- like stained (coloured) bodies seen in nucleus.

The text explains that the nucleosomes in chromatin are seen as ‘beads-on-string’ structure when viewed under electron microscope (EM) (Figure 5.4 b).

The text explains that the beads-on-string structure in chromatin is packaged to form chromatin fibers that are further coiled and condensed at metaphase stage of cell division to form chromosomes.

The text explains that but the question of what molecule was actually the genetic material, had not been answered.

The text explains that answer to some of the questions such as, why DNA is the predominant genetic material, whereas RNA performs dynamic functions of messenger and adapter has to be found from the differences between chemical structures of the two nucleic acid molecules.

The text explains that if one examines each requirement one by one, because of rule of base pairing and complementarity, both the nucleic acids (DNA and RNA) have the ability to direct their duplications.

The text explains that the other molecules in the living system, such as proteins fail to fulfill first criteria itself.

The text explains that this now can easily be explained in light of the DNA that the two strands being complementary if separated by heating come together, when appropriate conditions are provided.

The text explains that (Detailed discussion about this requires understanding of the process of repair in DNA, and you will study these processes in higher classes.) Both DNA and RNA are able to mutate.

The text explains that rNA can directly code for the synthesis of proteins, hence can easily express the characters.

The text explains that 5.3 RNA WORLD From foregoing discussion, an immediate question becomes evident – which is the first genetic material?

The text explains that it shall be discussed in detail in the chapter on chemical evolution, but briefly, we shall highlight some of the facts and points.

The text explains that there is now enough evidence to suggest that essential life processes (such as metabolism, translation, splicing, etc.), evolved around RNA.

The text explains that rNA used to act as a genetic material as well as a catalyst (there are some important biochemical reactions in living systems that are catalysed by RNA catalysts and not by protein enzymes).

The text explains that dNA being double stranded and having complementary strand further resists changes by evolving a process of repair.

The text explains that 5.4 REPLICATION While proposing the double helical structure for DNA, Watson and Crick had immediately proposed a scheme for replication of DNA.

The text explains that matthew Meselson and Franklin Stahl performed the following experiment in 1958: (i) They grew E. coli in a medium containing 15NH4Cl (15N is the heavy isotope of nitrogen) as the only nitrogen source for many generations.

The text explains that this heavy DNA molecule could be distinguished from the normal DNA by centrifugation in a cesium chloride (CsCl) density gradient (Please note that 15N is not a radioactive isotope, and it can be separated from 14N only based on densities).

The text explains that which property of DNA double helix led Watson and Crick to hypothesise semi-conservative mode of DNA replication?

The text explains that depending upon the chemical nature of the template (DNA or RNA) and the nature of nucleic acids synthesised from it (DNA or RNA), list the types of nucleic acid polymerases.

The text explains that how did Hershey and Chase differentiate between DNA and protein in their experiment while proving that DNA is the genetic material?

The text explains that differentiate between the followings: (a) Repetitive DNA and Satellite DNA (b) mRNA and tRNA (c) Template strand and Coding strand 9.

The text explains that list two essential roles of ribosome during translation.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5: Principles of Inheritance

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
